coredata.dc:title: A universal strategy to separate hydrophilic hybrid-light carbon quantum dots using pure water as eluent
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Huazhang
Wen, Shikai
Li, Weitao
Li, Ming
Wang, Liang
Chang, Qing
Zhang, Jiye
Lai, Jiawei
Vajtai, Robert
Ajayan, Pulickel M.
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: A sustainable separation technique is a key to obtaining high-purity monochrome fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have broad potential applications in light-emitting diodes, bioimaging, and biomedicine. In this work, a green, tenable, and universal method of effectively separating a large number of hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs using pure water as the eluent was demonstrated for the first time. Just after one separation, multicolor fluorescent CQDs can be divided into two, three, and four CQDs with a gradient of fluorescent colors ranging from blue to orange under UV light irradiation, in accordance with the increasing size of CQDs. The larger red-shift of the fluorescent color of CQDs before separation, the more the quantity of fluorescent CQDs were obtained after separation. Comparing with multicolor CQDs, the separated CQDs reveal similar structure characteristics, higher color-purity, narrower full width at half maximum, and better excitation-independent performance. This separation strategy relies on the adsorption capacity-size effect. Our finding opens the door to make separating hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs practical and straightforward, which will expand the potential applications of hydrophilic CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hydrophilic
Hybrid-light
Separate
Pure water

We thank the Laboratory for Microstructures of Shanghai University. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a, Chemical structure of nineteen precursors for hydrophilic CQDs preparation. b, One-pot synthesis route of nineteen CQDs. c, The separating procedure of multicolor CQDs.
Photographs of nineteen hydrophilic CQDs under visible light (a), UV light (b) and mixed with toluene at an equal volume ratio under visible light (c).

UV–vis absorption (blue), PL (black), and PLE (red) spectra (a–c) (insets: the photographs of the CQDs solution under visible (left) and UV light (right) irradiation) and TEM images (d–f) (inset: lateral size distribution diagram) of CQD1 (a, d), CQD2 (b, e), and CQD3 (c, f) before and after separation.

UV–vis absorption (blue), PL (black), and PLE (red) spectra (a–d) (insets: the photographs of the CQDs solution under visible (left) and UV light (right) irradiation) and TEM images (e–h) (inset: lateral size distribution diagram) of hybrid-light CQD4 before and after separation.

Images of four separated CQDs from CQD5 under visible (a) and UV light (b). (c) Corresponding PL spectra of separated CQDs. (d–g) PL spectra of separated CQDs excited at different wavelengths. (h–k) TEM images of separated CQDs (inset: lateral size distribution diagram). (l) Trend of lateral size (red) and transient fluorescence lifetime (green) of separated CQDs. FT-IR (m), XPS survey (n) and XRD pattern (o) spectra of CQDs before and after separation.
Schematic diagram of the separation mechanism of CQDs via column chromatography by pure water.

head.ce:title: A universal strategy to separate hydrophilic hybrid-light carbon quantum dots using pure water as eluent

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A universal strategy to separate hydrophilic hybrid-light carbon quantum dots using pure water as the eluent.

A sustainable separation technique is a key to obtaining high-purity monochrome fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have broad potential applications in light-emitting diodes, bioimaging, and biomedicine. In this work, a green, tenable, and universal method of effectively separating a large number of hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs using pure water as the eluent was demonstrated for the first time. Just after one separation, multicolor fluorescent CQDs can be divided into two, three, and four CQDs with a gradient of fluorescent colors ranging from blue to orange under UV light irradiation, in accordance with the increasing size of CQDs. The larger red-shift of the fluorescent color of CQDs before separation, the more the quantity of fluorescent CQDs were obtained after separation. Comparing with multicolor CQDs, the separated CQDs reveal similar structure characteristics, higher color-purity, narrower full width at half maximum, and better excitation-independent performance. This separation strategy relies on the adsorption capacity-size effect. Our finding opens the door to make separating hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs practical and straightforward, which will expand the potential applications of hydrophilic CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely used in photocatalysis [1–5], electrocatalysis [6,7], biosensing [8–11], bioimaging [12–16], and other fields [17–22] because of their low cost, facile functionalization, tunable fluorescence emission, good photostability, and suitable biocompatibility. Due to their values, multiple strategies have been developed to synthesize CQDs [23–28]. However, the produced CQDs didn’t exhibit satisfactory optical properties owing to uncontrollable preparation conditions of CQDs. For instance, the CQDs demonstrated broad photoluminescence (PL) spectra with a wide full width at half maximum (FWHM), excitation-dependence characteristics, and inferior color-purity [29–33], which have hindered the applications of high color-purity CQDs [34]. It is essential to purify and separate CQDs thoroughly to develop innovative applications.

To investigate CQDs with superior optical features, many separation processes have been attempted to isolate CQDs, such as polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) [35], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [36,37], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [38–42] and column chromatography [22,33,43–47]. Although it is easier to identify the fluorescent color and mobility of CQDs using PAGE route, the low separation efficiency of PAGE route limits its further large-scale applications. Meanwhile, the treatment capacity of the CE method is so low that it can merely handle a small amount of CQDs, which makes it difficult to collect fractions in CQDs separation. Furthermore, the HPLC seems to be a very successful technique with high separation efficiency, but this method needs expensive equipment. On the contrary, column chromatography is regarded as a low-cost and high-efficiency technique, which is the ideal choice to separate CQDs and collect CQDs’ fractions easily. Some previously published reports claimed that the CQDs solution could be separated by this method using different eluents [22,33,44,45], such as a hybrid eluent of ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, and methanol, and different concentrations of hydrochloric acid for CQDs. Unfortunately, it is complex and difficult to modulate the appropriate volume ratio of various organic eluents, even strong acid during the purification process. Even worse, because the experiment needs to be repeated several times, plenty of time and reagents are consumed in the process and result in low separation productivity. As a result, it is strongly desired to use a green, simple, and universal strategy for separation CQDs via column chromatography using an environmentally friendly solvent as eluent.

Herein, the first development of a separation approach for hydrophilic CQDs using pure water as eluent is presented. Nineteen novel hydrophilic CQDs were used as separation objects, which prepared via a common one-pot hydrothermal route using a series of precursors and NaOH as a reaction catalyst. After simple separation, five multicolor CQDs can be divided into two, three, and four different color CQDs effectively. The optical, morphology and structural properties of CQDs before and after separation were investigated and compared, and the possible separation mechanisms using pure water as an eluent are considered. Our finding supplies a convenient way to obtain high color-purity CQDs with outstanding optical performances, which may increase widespread applications of CQDs.

In a typical experiment, CQDs were prepared from precursors through a common hydrothermal route. The nineteen precursors are listed in Scheme 1 a. As showed in Scheme 1b, first, 0.05 g precursor1 was dissolved into 10 mL deionized water in an ultrasonic reactor for 5 min. Then 0.5 mL NaOH (1 mol/L) solution was added in the above solution, and the mixed solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave. After heating at 180 °C for 12 h, the autoclaves were naturally cooled down to room temperature. Finally, the product containing water-soluble CQD1 was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbon products. CQD2-19 were prepared by a similar procedure using corresponding precursor2-19. The pH value of the prepared CQDs solutions were adjusted from alkaline to neutral with 1 M HCl aqueous solution for further separation. The CQDs supernatant was subsequently dialyzed with a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000 Da) for several days and dried in an oven at 80 °C for characterization. Besides, the hydrophobic CQDs were produced by a one-pot solvothermal method using the same precursors and only toluene as a reaction solvent at the same reaction condition.

Before silica column separation, thin-1ayer chromatography was used to simulate a silica gel column for observing the preliminary separation effect of CQDs. In short, a small amount of CQDs solutions with neutral pH value were taken up by capillary and pointed them on the chromatographic sheets, and then make them climb up along the sheets using pure water as eluent. Finally, the climbing trend of CQDs on the sheets was observed under visible and UV light irradiation.

According to the results of thin-1ayer chromatography, the CQDs solution was purified and separated by silica gel column with the wet loading column, as showed in Scheme 1c. At first, 300–400 mesh silica gel was filled in the column and compacted. Secondly, a piece of cotton was put above the silica gel for preventing to flush out of silica gel when the addition of elution agent, which was infiltrated by pure water. Then, the neutral CQDs solution was filled into the top of the column, and the purified water was employed to elute the column. At last, all separated color solutions were determined under a portable UV lamp irradiation and collected during the separation process.

UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were acquired on a Hitachi 3010 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from the JEOL JEM-2100 F electron microscope. Zeta potentials of CQDs were measured at room temperature using a zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano, ZS90). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by 300 W AlKα radiation on an AMICUS electronic spectrometer on SHIMADZU. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Bio-Rad FT-IR spectrometer FTS165. An X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was generated with Rigaku D/max-2500 using CuKa radiation. The time-resolved PL spectra were measured on an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer.

The colors of nineteen CQD1-19 solutions can be seen to range from light yellow to brownish red under visible light (Fig. 1 a). These CQDs can emit various bright fluorescence ranging from blue to orange under UV light irradiation (Fig. 1b), which is a benefit for analyzing the separation effect. To explore the role of NaOH in the CQDs fusion process, a serious of controlled experiments were completed using the same precursors and without NaOH in the reaction. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescence of all the CQDs are blue and weak-green under UV-light irradiation, illustrating that the CQDs prepared under alkaline condition is a benefit for red-shifting their fluorescence and enhancing their fluorescence intensity. Moreover, the solutions of CQD1-19 were mixed with toluene at an equal volume ratio to verify their hydrophilic characteristics. As shown in Fig. 1c, it was observed that the water and organic phase were separated distinctly, and the organic phase remained transparent, indicating all the produced CQDs were hydrophilic.

Before silica column separation, thin-1ayer chromatography was used to simulate a silica gel column for observing the preliminary separation effect of CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, S3, CQD6-19 displayed only single-dot or a single-color track on the chromatographic sheets, whereas CQD1-5 exhibited a long trajectory due to their various composition, which illustrates the CQD1-5 are hybrid-light CQDs. The remaining single-color CQD6-19 can be emitted blue and green fluorescent under UV light irradiation (Fig. S4). Then, the selected hybrid-light CQD1-5 were further separated via silicon column chromatography using pure water as the eluent, which can be divided into two (CQD1-3), three (CQD4), and four (CQD5) different color CQDs ranging from blue to orange (Figs. 2–4
). Additionally, the hydrophobic CQDs were also used to measure the applicability of the pure water eluent in the separation process. As shown in Fig. S5a, the water and toluene phase were divided, and the CQDs remained in the toluene phase, revealing the produced CQDs were hydrophobic. In opposite to hydrophilic CQDs, all of the hydrophobic CQDs exhibited sole single-dot on the chromatographic sheets (v S5b, c), demonstrating the water eluent is unsuitable for separating the hydrophobic CQDs.

The multicolor CQD1-3 were separated into two different color CQDs via column chromatography using pure water as the eluent (Fig. 2). The collected fractions of separated CQD1-3 can emit brilliant fluorescent exciting at UV light, exhibiting green, yellow (a), cyan, green (b) and blue, green (c) fluorescence showed in Fig. 2a–c. As shown in Fig. 2a, the CQD1 before and after separation had similar excitonic absorption bands in the 250–300 nm region corresponded to the π–π* transitions of an aromatic system [49,50]. The maximal emission and excitation peaks of multicolor CQDs before and after separation are listed in Table S1. The maximal emission wavelength of cyan-light CQD1 was located at 530 nm, with 465 and 535 nm of green and yellow fluorescent separated CQDs, respectively, which is similar to that of CQD2 and CQD3 after separation. The maximal emission peak of CQD2 and CQD3 can be centered at 470 and 460 nm, and after separation, they were observed to be 465, 535, and 445, 540 nm, respectively. This phenomenon discovers that maximal emission wavelength of CQDs is between that of separated CQDs. Notably, the PL FWHM of the separated CQDs reveals narrower than that of the pre-separated CQDs in Table S2. The FWHM of CQDs1 was 68 nm, and 56 and 57 nm were obtained after separation. Meanwhile, separated CQD2 with an FWHM of 82 and 65 nm was narrower than that of original CQD2 (87 nm), which has the same trend of CQD3 after separation.

The morphology changes of the CQD1-3 before and after separation was further characterized by TEM measurements. As clearly shown in the TEM image (Fig. 2d–f), all of the CQDs were monodisperse. The average diameters of the CQD1 were 1.91 nm and 1.85, 2.06 nm after separation, respectively. Meanwhile, the average sizes of CQD2 before and after separation were 1.87 nm and 1.79, 1.96 nm, respectively, which is like the size trend of CQD3. The phenomenon indicated that the average size of CQDs is between that of separated CQDs, clarifying the hybrid-light CQDs are constituted with the CQDs of different sizes.

The FT-IR spectra shown in Fig. S6a–c were carried out to characterize the chemical bonds and functional groups in the CQD1-3 before and after separation. They exhibit the same functional groups on their surface of the CQD1 before and after separation, which are similar to the results of CQD2 and CQD3. Furthermore, the data of the XPS survey spectra further confirm the results of FT-IR spectra (Fig. S6d–f), revealing their same element compositions. Note that there is a slight change of the element ratio of CQDs before and after separation (Table S3) because various functional groups as shell dope on the surface of carbon-core, when these groups with different ratios occupy all active sites on the carbon-core surface, then the carbon-core stop growing and form different size CQDs. At the same time, the lattice parameters of the (002) crystal planes of CQD1-3 before and after separation varies in their XRD pattern spectra (Fig. S6g–i) due to the perturbation of different proportions functional groups of CQDs.

Similarly, three CQDs with blue, green, and yellow fluorescence were obtained after separating hybrid-light CQD4 using pure water as eluent in Fig. 3. All the samples displayed similar absorption bands with the center at about 325 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transitions of the CC bond. The fluorescence spectra of CQD4 before and after separation (Fig. 3a–d) also showed excitonic emission peaks centered at 536, 395, 455, and 535 nm, respectively. The FWHM of the separated CQDs were 68, 74, and 70 nm, respectively, which is much narrower than that of the CQD4 (80 nm, as shown in Table S2). The average sizes of CQD4 were 1.95 nm, and the separated fractions exhibited some significant differences in their size distribution according to TEM images in Fig. 3e–h. The average sizes of the separated CQDs were 1.65, 2.0, and 2.48 nm, respectively. The four identical chemical groups of CQD4 before and after separation, including NH (3223 cm−1), CO (1689 cm−1), CN (1489 cm−1) and COH (1069 cm−1), were shown in Fig. S7a. The analyzed conclusions of the XPS spectrum of CQD4 after separation (Fig. S7b) consist of that of their XRD patterns spectra (Fig. S7c), which are like that of CQD1-3.

The hybrid-light CQD5 previously reported by our group [48] emitted bright and stable orange fluorescent under UV light, as shown in Fig. 1a and b. The absorption peak was located at 280 and 440 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S8). Its optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were 510 and 590 nm, respectively. After simple separation, the CQD5 can be divided into four various color CQDs. And those CQDs after separation could be observed tunable fluorescent ranging from green to orange-red under UV light irradiation (Fig. 4a, b). As shown in Fig. S9, the absorption peak of the four high-color-purity fractions was observed at 370 (g-CQD5), 375 (y-CQD5), and 450 nm (o- and or-CQD5), respectively. The corresponding maximal emission wavelength were 500, 530, 590, and 640 nm (Fig. 4c), whereas the corresponding maximal PL excitation (PLE) peak was 370, 380, 450 and 520 nm (Fig. S10), respectively, which is consistent with the relevant excitonic absorption band, revealing that the emission of CQDs originates from band-edge exciton-state decay rather than defect-state decay [51]. The tendency of FWHM of CQD5 after separation has closely resembled CQD1-4. Additionally, as displayed in Fig. 4d–g, the PL emission peak of these separated CQDs did not shift with the different excitation wavelength, exhibiting more robust excitation-independence behavior of separated CQDs than CQD5 (20 nm red-shift). These phenomena further suggest it is a way to obtain high color-purity narrow FWHM CQDs through column chromatography separation using pure water as the eluent.

In the light of TEM images (Fig. 4h–k), the sizes of the separated fractions are 2.4, 2.7, 2.8, and 3.0 nm, respectively. The increased size of the separated CQDs is well consistent with gradually corresponding red-shifted narrow maximal emission peak significantly (Fig. 4l). The results demonstrate the quantum confinement effects of separated CQD5. Furthermore, the mono-exponential lifetime of the g-, y-, o- and or-CQD5 were determined to be 1.1, 2.3, 2.4, and 3.3 ns, respectively (Fig. S11). As the increasing size of the separated CQDs, the fluorescence lifetime is also prolonged with red-shift of maximal emission wavelength. To further study the role of functional groups, various spectroscopy characterizations were employed to investigate the structure of CQD5 before and after separation. Fig. 4m showed the FT-IR spectra of the five CQDs, which have the same stretching vibration for OH (3430 cm−1), NH (3220 cm−1), CO (1630 cm−1), CC (1445 cm−1), COH (1140 cm−1) and CN (1030 cm−1). The changes in their XPS and XRD data were consistent with that of CQD1-4 after separation. Therefore, the similar structure and chemical compositions indicate that the optical properties of the CQD1-5 before and after separation are dominated by their sizes due to the quantum confinement effect.

To illustrate the broad applicability of our separation strategy, small-molecular compounds and the high-molecular polymers from real samples are also used to prepare CQDs at the same procedure, such as lignin, polysaccharides of fruit extracts (apple, banana, and orange), protein (milk). The CQDs using lignin and apple juice as precursors revealed only single-dot or a single track on the chromatographic sheets (Fig. S12b), expressing they are single-color. Conversely, the CQDs synthesized using banana, orange, and milk displayed a long trajectory because of their complex parts on the chromatographic sheets (Fig. S12b). These CQDs can be divided into two-colors by column chromatography using pure water as the eluent (Fig. S12c), further verified that they are mix-light. Summarily, our separation technique is universal to CQDs’ purification and separation (Fig. 5 ).

The general principle of column chromatography separation is according to the polarity of the substance on the silica gel. The adsorption capacity of high-polarity substance on the silica column is stronger than that of low-polarity substance, so the low-polarity substance is eluded firstly. However, water is one of the highest polarity substances. In terms of the classic polarity mechanism of separation column chromatography, if the process uses pure water as the eluent, the mixture wouldn’t be separated. Therefore, the traditional polarity mechanism seems not to explain our aqueous-phase separation system of hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs. The phenomenon was observed that when water as the eluent flowed past the silica gel, the blue fluorescent CQDs with smaller sizes were always separated firstly, then the remaining CQDs sequentially appeared in accordance with their red-shift fluorescence, which is consisted with their increasing size (Figs. 2–4). Moreover, the zeta potential of hybrid-light CQD1-5 is unchanged after separation (Table S4). So, the separation mechanism is the adsorption capacity-size effect of CQDs via silica gel using pure water as the eluent.

In summary, a green sustainable, effective, and universal approach using pure water as the eluent was first displayed to separate series of hydrophilic hybrid-light CQDs. These novel hydrophilic CQDs were prepared via a common one-pot hydrothermal route using a variety of precursors. After simple separation, five multicolor CQDs can be divided into two, three, and four different colors CQDs. Compared with the optical properties of multicolor CQDs, the separated CQDs displayed outstanding optical performances, such as higher color-purity, narrower FWHM, and better excitation-independent behaviors. Moreover, the maximal emission wavelength of separated CQDs was red-shift with increasing their sizes, which display the quantum confinement effects. The higher red-shift the fluorescent color of CQDs before separation, the more the quantity of separated CQDs obtained after separation. Significantly, the separation mechanism is contributed to the adsorption capacity-size effect of CQDs via silica gel using pure water as the eluent. These investigations have already succeeded as a proof of concept, and provide a convenient and broad-applicability purification pathway for researchers in QDs-related fields.

L. Wang, M. H. Wu and P. M. Ajayan conceived the idea, wrote the paper, and provided input with data analysis and discussion. H. Z. Guo, M. Li, and W. T. Li realized material synthesis. H. Z. Guo performed CQDs separation, optical, morphology and structural measurements and analysis. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21671129, 21901154, 21571124, 21671131), the Shanghai Sailing Program (No. 16YF1404400), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT17R71). We thank the Laboratory for Microstructures of Shanghai University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2019.100528.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
